chain_of_thought: Ghidra MCP is unreachable so I cannot decompile libtbxml.so; I must base reasoning purely on crash metadata.
The native stack shows strchr_default -> decodeBytes -> Java_..._jniParse, so the first app-level frame is decodeBytes in libtbxml.so; strchr_default is a libc helper.
strchr_default is typically called by strlen, strchr, strdup, or similar; many XML parsers use C strings and assume null-terminated input.
The JNI bridge is jniParse([B)J, taking a raw byte array from Java; fuzz harness fuzz_one_input clearly feeds arbitrary bytes, including non-UTF-8, embedded NULs, or missing NUL terminators.

A crash in strchr_default usually comes from passing it a bad pointer (e.g., NULL or corrupted) rather than just weird contents, because strchr scans until it finds NUL but that normally only causes OOB read if pointer or length metadata was already wrong.

decodeBytes likely converts the Java byte[] to an internal C string or XML buffer; a common unsafe pattern is to treat the Java byte[] as a C string without adding a NUL or without ensuring bounds, then pass it into strchr/strlen.
However, I do not see allocator/sanitizer frames or explicit signals (SEGV vs abort) so I infer generic crash in strchr_default, likely from invalid pointer.

Is pointer attacker-controlled? The byte[] contents are attacker-controlled, but the pointer itself is allocated in native code; corruption would require decodeBytes to write out-of-bounds based on sizes derived from attacker bytes (e.g., parsing length fields or scanning for markers).

Given this is a minimal XML parsing library, it is plausible that decodeBytes walks the buffer and searches for characters using strchr without checking bounds and may read beyond or mis-handle malformed input, leading to invalid pointer arithmetic and eventual crash in strchr.

The Java call graph shows jniParse is reached from PluginKmlOverlay.loadKml(String) via TBXML.parse(String), which in real app reads KML from network/files; so inputs are realistically attacker-controlled.

Even though I cannot prove the exact pattern, a crash deep in libc strchr invoked from a parser on untrusted bytes with a very simple native library strongly suggests an out-of-bounds read/write or invalid pointer due to missing bounds checks in decodeBytes, not a harness-only artifact.
No evidence that fuzz harness passed impossible arguments (JNI signature matches: it expects a byte[] and receives one).

Therefore this is likely a real memory-safety bug in libtbxml’s decodeBytes handling of arbitrary byte arrays, reachable via KML loading; exploitable at least as DoS, possibly more if heap corruption occurs.
Without code I cannot assign a specific CWE beyond generic out-of-bounds/invalid pointer, but strchr-based crashes typically map to CWE-787/125.
Severity: at least medium (remote DoS through crafted KML); could be high if further analysis confirms heap corruption, but I stay at medium with moderate confidence.
reasons: Crash occurs in libc strchr_default called from libtbxml.so::decodeBytes while processing attacker-influenced byte[] through JNI
JNI bridge jniParse([B)J exposes raw byte buffer from Java without evidence of validation before native parsing
PluginKmlOverlay.loadKml(String) suggests XML/KML content may be remote or file-based input, making this path realistically attacker-reachable
strchr-based crashes in parsers on fuzzed binary input are commonly due to missing bounds checks / invalid pointer arithmetic rather than harness-only misuse

evidence.note: First app-level native frame below strchr_default; likely performing unsafe C-string style scanning on attacker-controlled bytes
JNI entry taking Java byte[]; no indication of bounds checking prior to calling decodeBytes
Java method exposes arbitrary byte array from higher-level XML/KML loader into native code

recommendations: Manually decompile and review libtbxml.so::decodeBytes to locate uses of strchr/strlen/memcpy that operate on buffers derived from Java byte[] without explicit length bounds
Add explicit length parameters and bounds checking to all string/byte scanning in decodeBytes instead of relying on implicit NUL termination
Add robust input validation in Java TBXML.parse/loadKml paths or disable native parsing for untrusted KML/XML until the native library is patched
assumptions: decodeBytes performs C-style string scanning and/or parsing on the Java-provided bytes
KML/XML content in PluginKmlOverlay.loadKml can be influenced by remote or untrusted sources
The crash in strchr_default is due to invalid pointer or OOB access originating from decodeBytes logic
limitations: Ghidra MCP backend was unreachable; no direct decompilation of libtbxml.so
Exact root cause (OOB read vs write vs NULL deref) cannot be confirmed from stack alone
No concrete signal/exception details (e.g., SIGSEGV vs SIGABRT) were provided

chain_of_thought: Native crash is in strpbrk, called from decodeBytes in libtbxml.so; we can’t decompile native due to MCP backend connection errors, so we infer from context. TBXML is a C XML/KML parser; decodeBytes likely converts a Java byte[] into a C string before parsing. strpbrk crashes typically when passed an invalid pointer (e.g., unterminated or corrupted string), suggesting memory misuse prior to the libc call.

Dataflow from Java: PluginKmlOverlay.loadKml reads arbitrary KML from network, files, or assets into a StringBuilder, then TBXML.parse(sb.toString()) → TBXML.jniParse(byte[]). That byte[] is directly derived from attacker-controlled KML content; there is no validation of encoding, embedded NULs, or length limits beyond standard Java constraints. The native side likely assumes well-formed, NUL-terminated text when using strpbrk.

Since the fuzz harness crash reproduces an actual app entry path (loadKml → TBXML.parse → jniParse), and the input in production can be remote URLs or external files, attacker control of the XML bytes is realistic. A crash in strpbrk due to malformed buffer or missing terminator implies an out-of-bounds read/write or invalid-pointer dereference in decodeBytes or earlier parsing logic. This meets the definition of a memory-safety vulnerability: untrusted input affects pointers/lengths used in a libc string routine without robust validation.

We lack exact C code for decodeBytes and Java_za_co_twyst_tbxml_TBXML_jniParse, so we can’t pinpoint the precise operation (e.g., missing NUL, incorrect buffer size, encoding mishandling). However, given the well-known TBXML pattern (C parser over caller-provided bytes) and that the only boundary between Java and native is the unvalidated byte[], the most plausible explanation is an input-dependent memory bug in decodeBytes or subsequent parsing. The fuzzer caused a crash in that function chain, reinforcing that malformed buffers can break assumptions.

Given realistic attacker control, native memory corruption/read via strpbrk, and the code’s role in processing KML from URLs or files, the issue is likely exploitable at least for denial of service and possibly for more severe corruption. Without disassembly we moderate confidence slightly but still classify as a likely genuine vulnerability.
reasons: Crash occurs in libc strpbrk called from app-native decodeBytes, implying unsafe string handling in native code.
Java passes attacker-controlled KML/XML content (from URLs/files) directly as a byte[] into jniParse with no bounds or format validation.
The crash path (loadKml → TBXML.parse → jniParse → decodeBytes → strpbrk) is a real app path, not a harness-only stub, so malformed external KML can realistically trigger the bug.

evidence.note: JNI bridge passes raw bytes from Java String into native TBXML parser without validation.
KML content is read from URL or file and fed directly into TBXML.parse, giving attackers control over native input.
Crash in C library string function suggests misuse of attacker-derived buffer in decodeBytes.

recommendations: Disassemble and audit Java_za_co_twyst_tbxml_TBXML_jniParse and decodeBytes to identify exact misuse of strpbrk and ensure all buffers are properly sized and NUL-terminated before C string operations.
Add robust input validation in Java and/or JNI (e.g., length checks, encoding validation) before handing attacker-controlled KML/XML to the native TBXML parser.
Consider replacing or sandboxing the TBXML native parser with a safer, bounds-checked implementation or a pure-Java XML/KML parser for untrusted data.
assumptions: decodeBytes operates directly on the byte[] from jniParse and uses strpbrk on that or derived buffers.
The crash is not due solely to an artificial fuzz harness constraint but reflects underlying unsafe handling in decodeBytes.
limitations: Ghidra MCP backend was unavailable, so native functions could not be decompiled to confirm exact buffer and length handling.
Exact crash instruction and register state at strpbrk call are unknown, so exploitability is inferred from typical failure modes.
